Who's Who in Riemann Hypothesis Research

Who's Who in Riemann Hypothesis Research

A directory of researchers working on the Riemann hypothesis

Bernhard Riemann (1826-1866) conjectured in his 1859 paper on the distribution of prime numbers that every nontrivial zero of the zeta function has real part exactly 1/2: they all lie on the critical line Re(s)=1/2. The conjecture is still open and is one of the Clay Millennium Prize Problems. More than a century and a half later, mathematicians around the world continue to attack it and the wider theory of the zeros of zeta and L-functions.

This site is a reference directory of those people. It catalogs the Top 100 researchers most active on the Riemann hypothesis and adjacent problems (the critical line, pair correlation of zeros, moments of the zeta function, Dirichlet L-functions, the Selberg class, and the random-matrix connection), and gives their institutions.

How the list is built

Three independent signals are combined into one composite ranking:

  1. arXiv preprint output since 2003, filtered to the math.NT category and matched against 15 Riemann-hypothesis search terms (Riemann hypothesis, Riemann zeta function, critical line, pair correlation, Dirichlet L-functions, and others).
  2. OpenAlex topical citations for the same Riemann-hypothesis phrases, capped at 10 authors per work to keep out physics megapapers.
  3. zbMATH Open, the curated mathematics review database, using the two RH-core MSC subject classes (11M26 zeros of zeta and L-functions and the Riemann hypothesis, 11M50 relations with random matrices).

The pipeline ranks are combined with a weighted order statistic. For each researcher the available ranks are sorted and weighted 70% on the best, 20% on the middle, and 10% on the worst, so being excellent in one Riemann-hypothesis pipeline counts most, while strength across all of them still wins overall. Lower is better. A researcher who appears in only one pipeline is not penalised; a missing rank is estimated from their nearest-ranked neighbours (the 70% weight only ever falls on a real, measured rank, never an estimate). The Riemann hypothesis is heavily an arXiv-preprint field, so the current ranking is dominated by the arXiv signal, with OpenAlex contributing where it overlaps; the zbMATH MSC layer is being folded in. False positives and off-topic authors are excluded by hand; the approach is described in the methodology.

Where to start

  • The Top 100 is the canonical ranked list, sortable in your browser.
  • Regional listings: North America, Europe, Asia & Pacific, Other regions.
  • Reading list: short Riemann-hypothesis papers for newcomers (in preparation).
  • Data: the ranked list as an open CC-BY dataset, with a citable DOI.
  • Methodology: how the data is built, audit decisions, and limitations.

Maintained by Steve Hubbard (admin@wwirh.org). Data sources: arXiv, OpenAlex, zbMATH Open, Mathematics Genealogy Project.

Last built June 2026.
